content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 20, 2015

Jannie L. Quinn
City Attorney
500 Castro Street
Mountain View, CA 94041

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-15-195

Dear Ms. Quinn:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Mountain View City
Councilmember Lenny Siegel regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

Councilmember Siegel owns and resides in a single-family two-story home in proximity to
the existing Downtown Transit Center. May Councilmember Siegel participate in governmental
decisions on a new Master Plan for the Transit Center and Caltrain Station ("Master Plan")
consisting of:

• Authorization of a professional services agreement with a consultant to outline the
planning process required to develop a Master Plan.

• Evaluation and selection of a roadway grade separation of the Castro Street intersection
at the Caltrain tracks and/or Central Expressway ("Grade Separation"), which actions
are necessary to inform the development of a Master Plan.

• Development of the Master Plan for the Transit Center based on the selection of the
Grade Separation option, consisting of potential improvements to the City's Downtown
Transit Center and Caltrain Station, a bus and shuttle loading area, bicycle and
pedestrian access and accommodation of increased commuter parking.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-195
Page No. 2

FACTS

The Master Plan: The Master Plan Process is a large-scale and comprehensive planning
effort to study and develop transportation improvements at the City's main transportation hub, the
Downtown Transit Center and Caltrain Station ("Transit Center"). The Transit Center is a key local
and regional facility, serving Caltrain commuter rail, Valley Transportation Authority (VTA) light
rail and buses, public and private shuttles, taxi service, bicycle parking and a regional bike station,
and all of these uses are expected to increase, based on various studies completed in 2014. In
response to significant increases in development and employment in the City, the volume of transit
usage, pedestrians and bicyclists in the area surrounding the Transit Center are anticipated to grow,
requiring expanded capacity and potential capital improvements at the Transit Center.

Specifically, Caltrain service at the Transit Center is expected to increase by 40 percent with
the completion of an electrification project in 2020. Demand for VTA light rail service is expected
to double at the Transit Center once express service to BART at the new Milpitas station becomes
as projected in 2017. Long term, high speed rail proposes to operate up to eight trains per hour in
the Peninsular rail corridor which would pass through the Transit Center.

The City will begin the Master Plan Process on October 27, 2015 by hiring a consultant to
outline the initial planning process required to develop the Master Plan, followed by an evaluation
of Grade Separation options at the major intersection of Castro/Moffett and Central Expressway
adjacent to the Transit Center followed by the development and implementation of the Master Plan.

The large scope of the project requires review of governmental decisions by the City
Council at various stages, with each decision informing the eventual adoption of a Master Plan. For
that reason, the property that is the subject of the governmental decision is defined by the
boundaries of the City's Downtown Transit Center and Caltrain Station, and encompasses all of the
improvements potentially authorized by the Master Plan. "Transit Center" includes the intersection
where the potential Grade Separation is located. Although the Master Plan Process will include
separate decision components for the grade separation and the expansion of the Transit Center, the
two potential improvements are inextricably linked as a comprehensive project.

Councilmember Siegel's Property: Councilmember Siegel owns and resides in a
single-family two-story home at 269 Loreto Street in the City's Old Mountain View neighborhood,
and is located 1,330 feet from the Project Site.

• The house is typical in terms of size and character to the majority of single-family homes in
the neighborhood and in fact the City overall. The Old Mountain View neighborhood
consists almost exclusively of owner occupied single family residences served by small,
narrow streets.

• Councilmember Siegel's home is separated diagonally from the Project Site and Grade
Separation by three and four large, densely populated city blocks respectively consisting
mostly of single family homes, in addition to several large multi-unit residential complexes
and some condominiums.

File No. A-15-195
Page No. 3

• The City's downtown area is made up of distinct areas with different intensities of
commercial and residential use. Because of the shortage of parking in the downtown core,
vehicles are often parked during the lunch and dinner hours on streets in Old Mountain
View. Commuters using the Transit Center also use these streets, and pedestrians and
bicyclists use the streets to access the Transit Center. However, streets used for this purpose
are generally located closer, and on a more direct route, than Councilmember Siegel's street
to the downtown core and the Transit Center.

• Shops and restaurants are clustered in the historic retail district which is surrounded by the
Old Mountain View residential neighborhood.

• Councilmember Siegel's property has the same limited development potential as all other
residences in the Old Mountain view neighborhood; he does not own multiple real property
economic interests in the area; his property size is typical for the neighborhood. Therefore,
there would be no unique effect on Councilmember Siegel's property.

Potential Effects of the Project

• Traffic: With the projected growth in Transit Center usage, the city anticipates a
corresponding increase in the number of pedestrians and bicyclists using the facility, both to
connect between transit services and to access adjacent streets, neighborhoods and
businesses. However, regional commuters are likely to access the Transit Center from other
cities using major streets, not traversing through Councilmember Siegel's immediate
neighborhood to access the Transit Center. The Grade Separation roadway improvements
may actually increase traffic efficiency and flow in the downtown area, potentially
improving traffic levels. Increased public and private shuttle bus service is expected with the
expanded usage of the Transit Center. However, Councilmember Siegel's property is not
located on a main thoroughfare or street which is used to access the Transit Center. In
addition, the increased numbers of visitors to the downtown area expected as a result of the
Transit Center expansion would likely not travel through Councilmember Siegel's
immediate neighborhood. No direct route exists from Councilmember Siegel's property to
the Transit Center.

• Intensity of Use: The Old Mountain View neighborhood is essentially built, and the historic
retail district with shops and restaurants is also essentially fully developed and not likely to
change regardless of the any changes in the intensity of use.

• Parking: Currently Caltrain commuter parking at the Transit Center is full throughout the
day. In addition, an estimated 150-200 commuters park their vehicles on streets adjacent to
the downtown, with a majority of this parking in the blocks closest to the Transit Center.
Future parking demand is estimated to increase to 800-1,000 spaces as the result of a
Caltrain electrification project. The Master Plan Process could identify the appropriate
number of parking spaces needed to accommodate projected increases in parking demand,
including the size of a potential parking structure. The Master Plan could also provide for
increased parking capacity in the form of a five parking lot and/or structure to mitigate
parking impacts which may potentially improve the commuter and visitor parking impacts

File No. A-15-195
Page No. 4

on neighborhood streets. Neighborhood street parking in the Old Mountain View
neighborhood could be impacted. The projected rise in transit usage will likely equate to
increased volume of visitors to downtown restaurants and shops. Commuter and visitor
parkers customarily park in the blocks and streets located closer to the Transit Center, and
visitors park closer to streets near Castro street (the main commercial street). While cars
park on Councilmember Siegel's street, they park toward Bush Street and do not reach his
block.

• View: Any Grade Separation option at road level or below at the intersection adjacent to the
Transit Center would have no impact on views. An elevated bicycle pedestrian overcrossing
may be a potential improvement resulting from the Master Plan. Councilmember Siegel's
home is situated on flat land with existing views limited to the areas immediately
surrounding it. From the upstairs of Councilmember Siegel's house, he cannot see a
four-story residential building that lies between his house and the Transit Center, an
apartment complex that is at least four stories that lies between his house and the Transit
Center or a twelve-story office building that is at a distance similar to the Transit Center.
There is no likelihood any Grade Separation option or improvements to the Transit Center
would impact Councilmember Siegel's view. Councilmember Siegel's home is situated on
flat land with existing views limited to the neighborhood surrounding it.

• Privacy, Noise & Air Quality or Other Factors: Train noise (horns, etc.) may be more
frequent due to increased numbers of trains passing through the Transit Center with its
expansion. However, Councilmember Siegel already is exposed to train noises (to the extent
such may be heard from his property). More shuttles and bus volume will access the Transit
Center. Many shuttles and buses are moving towards zero or low emissions, so air quality
and noise impacts resulting from these vehicles would be minimal. There will also be
increased pedestrian and bicycle travel to and from the Transit Center, but travel routes of
each are usually along main thoroughfares, not though the center of the residential area
Councilmember Siegel's property is located. No facts suggests privacy will be impacted by
any aspect of the project.2

ANALYSIS AND CONCLUSIONS

Section 87100 prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in
making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official
has a financial interest specified in Section 87103. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
government decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section
87103.)

Councilmember Siegel has an interest in his real property, which he owns. (Section
87103(b).)

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is
"explicitly involved" in the decision. Real property is "explicitly involved" in a decision if "the

2 You also provided data related to the "public generally" analysis. Since we did not reach that exception we
do not include the facts herein.

File No. A-15-195
Page No. 5

decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6)."
(Regulation 18701(a).) The councilmember's real property is not explicitly involved in the
decisions. Thus, the following foreseeability analysis applies: "A financial effect need not be likely
to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the
financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public
official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable." (Regulation 18701(b).)

Regulation 18702.2(a) provides a list of circumstances under which the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on real property in which an official has a
financial interest is material. As relevant to your facts, the financial effect will be material if the
decisions:

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial
interest;

* * *

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the
market value of the official's property."

Councilmember Siegel's home is 1,640 feet from the project site and further from the
intersection in question. You stated that it would be unlikely that the councilmember's property
would be affected for the following reasons:

• Traffic: Regional commuters are likely to access the Transit Center from major streets and
not traversing through Councilmember Siegel's immediate neighborhood to access the
Transit Center.

• The Grade Separation: Councilmember Siegel's property is not located on a main
thoroufare or street which is used to access the Transit Center and the increased numbers
of visitors to the downtown area expected as a result of the Transit Center expansion would
likely not travel through Councilmember Siegel's immediate neighborhood. No direct route
exists from Councilmember Siegel's property to the Transit Center.

• Intensity of Use: The Councilmember's neighborhood is essentially built, and the historic
retail district with shops and restaurants is also essentially fully developed and not likely to
change regardless on any change to the intensity of use.

File No. A-15-195
Page No. 6

• Parking: Neighborhood street parking in the councilmember's neighborhood could be impacted but the impact would not likely be so substantial that it would affect the value of the councilmember's home. Moreover, while the projected rise in transit usage will likely equate to increased volume of visitors to downtown restaurants and shops, current cars do not park on the councilmember's end of the street (they park toward Bush Street and do not reach his block).

• View: There is no likelihood any Grade Separation option or improvements to the Transit Center would impact Councilmember Siegel's view. Councilmember Siegel's home is situated on flat land with existing views limited to the neighborhood surrounding it.

• Privacy, Noise & Air Quality or Other Factors: Train noise (horns, etc.) may be more frequent due to increased numbers of trains passing through the Transit Center with its expansion. However, Councilmember Siegel already is exposed to train noises (to the extent such may be heard from his property). More shuttles, bus and increased pedestrian and bicycle traffic could increase the general noise levels in the area, however, travel routes are usually along main thoroughfares, not though the center of the residential area Councilmember Siegel's property is located. No facts suggests privacy will be impacted by any aspect of the project.

Based upon the facts provided, it appears that traffic, parking, and the intensity of use would be the most substantially affected by the decisions in question. However, the effect on each of these items is mitigated by the fact that the councilmember does not live on a major throughway. Additionally, changes to the existing transit center are unlikely to have significant affects on the councilmember's neighborhood, which is already fully developed. In this case, it is not foreseeable that the decisions in question will materially affect the councilmember's property.

Public Generally Exception: You also asked about the application of the public generally exception as to other related decisions. However, since we have concluded the councilmember does not have a conflict of interest in the specific questions, we need not apply the public generally exception.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel,
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

sections.analysis:
AND CONCLUSIONS 
Section 87100 prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in 
making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official 
has a financial interest specified in Section 87103. A public official has a "financial interest" in a 
government decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision 
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 
87103.) Councilmember Siegel has an interest in his real property, which he owns. (Section 
87103(b).) 
Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is 
"explicitly involved" in the decision. Real property is "explicitly involved" in a decision if "the 
+ You also provided data related to the "public generally" analysis, Since we did not reach that exception we 
do not include the facts herein.  
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or 
other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation l 8702.2(a)(l )-(6)." 
(Regulation 18701 (a).) The councilmember's real property is not explicitly involved in the 
decisions. Thus, the following foreseeability analysis applies: "A financial effect need not be likely 
to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the 
financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public 
official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable." (Regulation 1870l(b).) 
Regulation 18702.2(a) provides a list of circumstances under which the reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on real property in which an official has a 
financial interest is material. As relevant to your facts, the financial effect will be material if the 
decisions: 
"(l 0) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest;
